coredata.dc:title: Red dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs
coredata.dc:creator: Li, He
Ye, Hong-Gang
Cheng, Rui
Guo, Jiazhuang
Liang, Zhi-Bin
Li, Ge
Li, Qing
Wang, Cai-Feng
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have been rapidly developed as a kind of luminescent nanomaterials. However, methods for the synthesis of long-wavelength-emissive carbon dots are still not mature enough. Meanwhile, developing a fast and highly sensitive method to detect residual veterinary drugs in food is particularly significant for food safety. Herein, we report the facile fabrication of red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs. With use of sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, R-CDs was prepared via a one-pot solvothermal approach. The resulting R-CDs show mainly excitation-independent and dual-emissive photoluminescence (PL) located at 597 and 645 nm. Meanwhile, the PL quantum yield (QY) of R-CDs without further surface treatment is 33% under 540 nm excitation. Interestingly, the R-CDs exhibit fast fluorescence response to malachite green oxalate (MG oxalate), 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride (AHD) and pefloxacin, which are abused widely and retained easily in animals. Ratiometric sensing of these veterinary drugs were successfully performed taking the advantage of dual-emissive PL property; the detection limits of MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin were 0.79, 0.25 and 0.85 μM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red dual emission
Fluorescence
Synthesis
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Veterinary drug

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of veterinary drugs and metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM characterization of R-CDs. Inset: HRTEM image of the corresponding CD. (b) XRD pattern, (c) Raman spectrum and (d) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of R-CDs. (b–d) High-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs.

(a) PL excitation and PL emission spectra of R-CDs (Inset: Digital pictures of R-CDs in ethanol taken under daylight and UV light, respectively) (b) UV–vis absorption of R-CDs. (c) Fluorescence decay of R-CDs. (d) Excitation-independent PL emission spectra of R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
(a) F1/F2 and (b) PL spectra of R-CDs in the presence of MG oxalate, AHD, pefloxacin and various metal ions.

PL spectra of R-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of (a) MG oxalate, (c) AHD, and (e) pefloxacin. Linear graphs of F1/F1, 0, F2/F2, 0, F1/F2 versus concentration of (b) MG oxalate, (d) AHD and (f) pefloxacin.
Schematic illustration of synthesizing R-CDs by solvothermal method and the application in detecting veterinary drugs.

head.ce:title: Red dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing, 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing, 210009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have been rapidly developed as a kind of luminescent nanomaterials. However, methods for the synthesis of long-wavelength-emissive carbon dots are still not mature enough. Meanwhile, developing a fast and highly sensitive method to detect residual veterinary drugs in food is particularly significant for food safety. Herein, we report the facile fabrication of red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs. With use of sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, R-CDs was prepared via a one-pot solvothermal approach. The resulting R-CDs show mainly excitation-independent and dual-emissive photoluminescence (PL) located at 597 and 645 nm. Meanwhile, the PL quantum yield (QY) of R-CDs without further surface treatment is 33% under 540 nm excitation. Interestingly, the R-CDs exhibit fast fluorescence response to malachite green oxalate (MG oxalate), 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride (AHD) and pefloxacin, which are abused widely and retained easily in animals. Ratiometric sensing of these veterinary drugs were successfully performed taking the advantage of dual-emissive PL property; the detection limits of MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin were 0.79, 0.25 and 0.85 μM, respectively.

Highly red dual-emissive carbon dots are synthesized from one-pot solvothermal treatment and show ratiometric fluorescence sensing toward veterinary drugs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of star photoluminescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been developed rapidly in recent years. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, such carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit outstanding properties [1–4], such as stable fluorescence characteristics, facile synthesis processes, excellent biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness, which make them potentially useful for sensing [1], bioimaging [5], printing [4], detection [6,7] and optoelectronic devices [8–10]. The preparation methods of CDs may be generally divided into two kinds: bottom-up and top-down and strategies. The bottom-up rotes mainly include hydrothermal, solvothermal and microwave assisted method [11–15]. The top-down synthesis methods generally include, electrochemical, arc discharge, chemical oxidation and laser ablation methods [16–18]. Despite great advances, issues remain in this field. For instance, most of the CDs exhibit a short-wavelength photoluminescence (PL) emission (λ＜550 nm) [1], drastically limiting their applications. There are still only a few reports on products with high-quality long-wavelength emission, especially those located in red light region [13,19–21], which is a big obstacle for the development of CDs. Therefore, it is highly needed to design simple and efficient synthetic methods to produce red-emissive CDs.

On the other hand, veterinary drugs are a series of medicines usually used to prevent and cure diseases in animals, especially in food animals [22]. However, the misapplication of veterinary drugs usually leads to the drug residue in foods, which is harmful to human health [23]. Therefore, it is particularly significant to exploit a fast and highly sensitive method to detect residual veterinary drugs in food. Up to now, the detection methods for residual veterinary drugs in foods have been widely reported, including thin layer chromatography (TLC) [24], high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [25], isotope dilution mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [26], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [27], surf-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), etc [28]. Nevertheless, alternative methods circumventing expensive equipment and cumbersome operation are still highly desirable, and it is of momentous practical significance to exploit detection methods for quickly and accurately detecting residual veterinary drugs in foods.

In recent years, ratiometric fluorescence sensing methods have been developed, which realize the detection of analytes based on the change in the ratio of PL intensities of two emission peaks [29,30]. Such methods could eliminate systematic errors and avoid environmental background interference, and hence improve the detection accuracy and sensitivity. CDs functionalized with rich surface groups can show photoluminescent or chemiluminescent response to external stimuli [6,7,11,31–33]. Especially, some CDs with dual emission have been developed to show potential for ratiometric sensing [2,30].

In this paper, we exhibit an efficient fabrication of red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) and their application in ratiometric detection toward veterinary drugs. By using sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine as precursors and undergoing a simple solvothermal treatment in ethanol, R-CDs with dual PL emission centered at 597 and 645 nm were obtained. The emission peak is excitation-independent and the absolute PL QY can reach 33% under the excitation of 540 nm without further surface treatment. Moreover, the R-CDs perform a fast fluorescence response to malachite green oxalate (MG oxalate), 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride (AHD), and pefloxacin, which are abused widely and retained easily in animals. Interestingly, ratiometric sensing of these veterinary drugs were successfully performed with better accuracy and sensitivity under the advantage of dual-emissive PL property, in which the detection limits of MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin were 0.79, 0.25 and 0.85 μM, respectively.

Sorbitol, o-phenylenediamine, and ethanol were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Malachite green oxalate (MG oxalate, C52H54N4O12), pefloxacin (C17H20FN3O3), 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride (AHD, C3H6ClN3O2), sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid and all kinds of inorganic metal salts mentioned in the experiments were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. All materials were used without further purification.

Hydrothermal method was chosen to synthesis R-CDs. 0.18 g of sorbitol and 0.33 of g o-phenylenediamine (with the molar ratio of sorbitol/o-phenylenediamine～1:3) were dispersed in 10 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled down to the room temperature, and the solution of R-CDs was obtained. In purification process, the large particles were removed by 0.22 μm millipore membrane, and the unreacted precursors and other undesired products were removed by dialysis (1000 Da of cut-off molecular weight) for 30 h. Finally, the brown R-CDs powder was obtained via concentration and freeze drying for further experiments and characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of veterinary drugs and metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the response of R-CDs to veterinary drugs, a series of 30 μL veterinary drug solutions with different concentrations were added separately to 3 mL of R-CDs solution with concentration of 100 μg mL−1. And PL intensity of the solutions with different concentrations of veterinary drugs were measured to evaluate the relationship between the concentration of veterinary drugs and the ratiometric PL intensity. Simultaneously, we also investigated the fluorescence response toward various metal ions (Na+, Ag+, K+, Li+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, In3+, and Fe3+) by a similar method, in which 1 mM aqueous solutions of various metal ions were prepared prior to use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of R-CDs were carried out by JEOL 1011 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested via ES-CAIAB250 XPS system. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrogram was characterized by Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. FLS-980 was used to characterize PL lifetime and absolute PL QY. Fluorescence maps were provided through Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were characterized through PerkinElmer Lambda 900 Ultraviolet–visible spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was shown with D5005 X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectra were measured by Jobin Yvon Horiba Labram Infinity.

The characterization of XPS was carried out to discuss the structural characteristics of R-CDs in depth. In Fig. 2 a, the full spectrum clearly displays three typical peaks at 285, 399 and 531.9 eV, respectively, indicating the existence of carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s) in R-CDs. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum contains three peaks located at 284.8, 285.9, and 287.8 eV, attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2b) [37,38]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks centered at 398.5, 399.1, and 400.2 eV, suggesting the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 2c) [21,35,39]. Fig. 2d exhibits the O 1s band with two peaks at 532.1, 533.4 eV, assigning to CO and C–O [40]. The results further confirm the existence of rich functional groups on the surface of R-CDs.

We then explored the optical properties of R-CDs. The PL excitation spectrum exhibits two peaks appeared at 540 and 573 nm, and the PL emission spectrum exhibits a dual-emissive PL centered at 597 and 645 nm (Fig. 3 a). The digital photographs of R-CDs dispersed in ethanol show a reddish brown solution under daylight and red fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). In UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs, two main absorptions were observed, one centered at 277 nm and the other wide one appeared in the range of 450–580 nm (Fig. 3b). The absorption at 277 nm might be assigned to the π-π* transition, which is originated from aromatic CC bond. The broad absorption band (450–580 nm) should be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 chemical bonds (CO and CN) [19]. To obtain the PL lifetime of R-CDs, the time-resolved PL decay was tested. The obtained fluorescence decay data were fitted by a biexponential decay function to work out the average PL lifetime of 3.2 ns (Fig. 3c). An excitation-independent PL behavior is observed for R-CDs that the emission wavelength keeps unchanged with change in excitation wavelength in the range of 450–570 nm (Fig. 3d). The strongest emission and the absolute PL QY of 33% were also recorded with the excitation wavelength of 540 nm.

In addition, the reaction parameters on the PL characters of R-CDs were thoroughly investigated. Fig. S2 exhibits the relationship between the PL emission wavelength and the molar ratio of sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine. It is obvious that the optimal PL emission wavelength is provided with the molar ratio of sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine of 1:3. Meanwhile, the maximum absolute PL QY of 33% was also recorded at the molar ratio of 1:3 (Fig. S3). Figs. S4 and S5 reveal the relationship between PL optical properties and reaction temperature. It is clearly that the reaction temperature of 200 °C gives the optimal PL emission wavelength and the maximum absolute PL QY. Similarly, Figs. S6 and S7 exhibit that the reaction time of 12 h is the most suitable reaction time. Thus, by thermal treating sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine (1:3 in molar ratio) at 200 °C for 12 h in ethanol, excitation-independent, red-emissive R-CDs with PL QY of 33% could be prepared directly, which lays a foundation for them in subsequent application.

The resultant R-CDs exhibit a natural red dual-emissive character, and more importantly, the intensities of the two emissive peaks increase or decrease synchronously when the surroundings of R-CDs are changed. The interesting feature makes R-CDs a potential candidate for ratiometric fluorescence detection. Significantly, when detecting target objects, such dual-emissive nanomaterials with ratiometric fluorescence sensing capacity show a superb merit of intrinsic built-in correction, which endows the sensor with an excellent anti-interference performance [2]. In this case, the R-CDs show fluorescent sensing to MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin with a rapid fluorescence response. Compared to traditional fluorescent sensors, the R-CDs may record the changes of fluorescence intensities with higher accuracy by the ratio of the double peaks (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a and b shows the fluorescence variation of R-CDs toward MG oxalate, AHD, pefloxacin and various metal ions (Na+, Li+, Pb2+, Ag+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, In3+, and Fe3+). The intensities of these two emission peaks centered at 597 nm and 645 nm are called as F1 and F2. The results exhibit that negligible fluorescence change occurs upon the incorporation of various metal ions with R-CDs; concentrated Fe3+ solution could diminish the PL intensity of R-CDs (Fig. S8). Whereas, the R-CDs show sensitive sensing toward MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin. The sensitive and selective response feature make R-CDs probably useful to detect these veterinary drugs.

The fluorescence variations of R-CDs toward MG oxalate, AHD, and pefloxacin were thoroughly investigated. Fig. 5 a shows the changes of PL intensities of R-CDs with various concentrations of MG oxalate from 0 to 300 μM. PL intensities of R-CDs at F1 and F2 both decrease with the increase of MG oxalate concentration. In Fig. 5b, as the MG oxalate concentration increases from 0 to 250 μM, the ratio of PL intensity at F1 to that at F2 (F1/F2) decreases linearly. The correlation coefficient R2 = 0.997 proves excellent correlation. The detection limit was determined to be 0.79 μM. The ratio of PL intensity at F1/F1, 0 and F2/F2, 0 (The PL intensity of emissive peak centered at 597 nm and 645 nm without veterinary drugs are called as F1, 0 and F2, 0) also exhibit linear relationship, but the corresponding R2 of 0.992 and 0.985 are smaller than that of F1/F2, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence detection with double peaks is superior to single peak fluorescence detection. Table S1 presents the detection of MG by different methods. This work paves an alternative way circumventing expensive equipment and cumbersome operation, to quickly and accurately detect target objects with wide detection range.

The fluorescence response data of detecting AHD is shown in Fig. 5c and d. As the AHD concentration increases from 0 to 500 μM, F1/F1, 0 F2/F2, 0, and F1/F2 decreases linearly, the linear relationship for ratio of PL intensity at F1/F2 gave a detection limit of 0.25 μM. Fig. 5e and f shows the results of detecting of pefloxacin. As the pefloxacin concentration increases from 0 to 900 μM, the ratio of PL intensity at F1/F2 decreases linearly, exhibiting a detection limit of 0.85 μM. Besides, in all three cases of PL response of R-CDs toward MG oxalate, AHD and pefloxacin, the dual-emissive ratiometric PL sensing (F1/F2) has higher accuracy in comparison with those of single PL peak sensing (F1/F1, 0 and F2/F2, 0), suggesting that R-CDs with merit of ratiometric sensing ability might have a wide application prospect in food safety.

The origin of dual emission in R-CDs, as well as the fluorescence quenching mechanism of R-CDs towards veterinary drugs, were proposed. As an aqueous solution of MG oxalate (1 mM, 30 μL) was added to the ethanol solution of R-CDs (100 μg mL−1, 3 mL), the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of R-CDs occurred (Fig. S9a). Whereas, no obvious PL peak shift was observed. Moreover, TEM measurements indicated negligible change in the size of CDs before and after the addition of MG oxalate (Figs. S9b and c). These features suggest that the addition of MG oxalate into the R-CD solution would not lead to aggregation of CDs. Aggregation is commonly responsible for fluorescence quenching of nanoparticles [41], but obviously it is not the quenching mechanism in this case. Furthermore, the incorporation of MG oxalate into the R-CD solution won't destroy the surface functional groups of R-CDs, as demonstrated by FT-IR and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopies (Figs. S9d and e). Fig. S9f shows the fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs before and after adding MG oxalate. No significant change in PL decay is observed, indicating that the radiative pathway of R-CDs keeps unchanged. In addition, we investigated the fluorescence behavior of R-CDs in different pH environments (Fig. S10). These excitation-independent and dual-emissive PL is relatively stable in the pH range of 6–11, but reduce sharply in the pH below 6, which is in good agreement with those reported previously [42,43]. This feature suggests that abundant functional groups on the surface of R-CDs probably play as fluorophores, which are probably the dominant contributors to dual emission of R-CDs at 597 and 645 nm [42,43]. As such, the dual emission of R-CDs sensitively responds to external stimuli. Veterinary drugs (MG oxalate, AHD, pefloxacin) containing functional groups (-COOH or –NH3 +), could interact with the fluorophores on R-CDs surface, leading to fluorescence response with diverse linear relationship. The different degree of fluorescence quenching was also found in the treatment of alanine with R-CDs (Fig. S11), confirming the above sensing mechanism. We also noted that the PL emission at 645 nm maintains unchanged below pH < 3, which might be ascribed to radiative recombination in carbogenic core [42]. These preliminary results suggest R-CDs could be applied as sensors in principle for the detection of veterinary drugs, while continuous work will be required for complex practical applications.

In summary, red dual-emissive R-CDs were synthesized to show ratiometric fluorescence sensing toward veterinary drugs. The reaction parameters of R-CDs were thoroughly investigated by adjusting the molar ratio of precursors, reaction temperature and time. The resulting R-CDs show dual-emissive photoluminescence (PL) centered at 597 and 645 nm, which are located in red light region. Under the excitation wavelength of 540 nm, the R-CDs without further surface treatment have the maximum absolute PL QY of 33%, which are useful for various application such as bioimaging and biolabeling. More importantly, the R-CDs with dual emission possess better accuracy and sensitivity for detection application because of the intrinsic built-in correction capacity. As such, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of R-CDs toward malachite green oxalate, 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride and pefloxacin were investigated, to give detection limit of 0.79, 0.25, and 0.85 μM, respectively. This work may be instructive for facile and rapid preparation of R-CDs, and contributes to the development of food safety inspection and biomedicine.

Cai-Feng Wang and Su Chen supervised the project. He Li, Hong-Gang Ye, and Rui Cheng performed the experiments, carried out characterizations, and analyzed the data. Jiazhuang Guo, Zhi-Bin Liang, Ge Li, and Qing Li contributed to the characterizations. He Li, Rui Cheng, Cai-Feng Wang and Su Chen contributed to the interpretation of the results and wrote the paper. All authors reviewed the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1602800 and 2016YFB0401700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21736006 and 21978132), the Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201704 and ZK201716), and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
